title: How Do Systems Prioritise? Queues, Stacks, and Emergency Triage
description: Students compare stack, queue, and priority queue behavior, then design triage logic where arrival order differs from urgency.

roleModelOfDay.name: Annie Easley
roleModelOfDay.intro: A NASA computer scientist who developed algorithms for energy systems and rocket launches — where the order in which a computer processes tasks can mean the difference between a successful mission and a disaster.

vocabulary.def: A data structure where the last item added is the first one removed (LIFO)
A data structure where the first item added is the first one removed (FIFO)
Last In, First Out — the stack processing order
First In, First Out — the queue processing order
A queue where items are processed by importance, not arrival order
A number representing how urgent or critical a case is

learningObjectives: Explain the difference between Stack and Queue processing using LIFO and FIFO behavior.
Identify when FIFO is appropriate and when it becomes dangerous in high-stakes systems.
Design a plain-language priority queue algorithm with severity and tie-breaking logic.
Evaluate how scoring and tie-break rules encode policy choices, not neutral technical facts.
fullDescription: Age group: 12-17. Duration: 30 minutes. SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Learning goal: students explain stack versus queue behavior and design a priority queue for emergency triage where order of importance can override order of arrival.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson. Write the triage scenario (patients A–D with arrival time, severity, and condition) on the board or project it. Have four books/notebooks ready at the front for the stack demonstration. Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
steps: Phase 1 (0-2 min): Ask spark question about self-driving car processing three simultaneous inputs and who decides priority.
Phase 1 hook: connect processor scheduling and emergency systems to real-world value-encoded prioritization.
Phase 2 (2-10 min): Demonstrate stack with four books and define LIFO. Contrast with queue examples and define FIFO.
Phase 2 reflection: ask why FIFO fairness can fail in hospitals where urgency is not arrival-based.
Phase 3 (10-15 min): Introduce priority queue with severity score, arrival tie-breaks, and reordering on new high-severity arrivals.
Phase 3 extension: introduce preemption question, whether high-priority arrivals interrupt currently processed lower-priority tasks.
Phase 4 (15-24 min): Emergency triage challenge in groups using patient dataset and algorithm rules.
Round 1 (3 min): initial order by severity expected as D, B, C, A and quick fairness check discussion.
Round 2 (5 min): add Patient E at 9:20 with severity 7, then update to severity 10 while treatment is in progress to trigger preemption decision.
Round 3 (2 min): add six simultaneous severity-8 arrivals and require explicit tie-breaking rule.
Phase 5 (24-28 min): discussion on preemption in safety-critical systems or values encoded in recommendation queues.
Exit reflection (28-30 min): students write responsibility statement about priority-queue power and weight-setting.
discussionPrompts: Where is preemption clearly right in everyday life, and where is it dangerous?
How should designers decide preemption policy in safety-critical systems such as surgical robotics?
Priority weights in social feeds optimize engagement. Who sets those weights and who is disadvantaged?
What is the ethical difference between triage prioritization and engagement prioritization if both are implemented with similar data structures?
tips: Use physical stack demo first so LIFO is embodied before formal naming.
Keep reminding students that fairness and safety can conflict in queue policy design.
Push groups to specify tie-break rules explicitly; unspecified ties cause inconsistent behavior.
Ask what system state the algorithm can see when making preemption decisions.
Reward students who identify weight-setting as policy, not just computation.

curriculumAlignment: Data Structures: Stack, Queue, Priority Queue
teacherGuide: Core message: ordering structures are decision systems. Stack and queue encode fixed processing logic, while priority queues encode weighted value judgments that must be justified, tested, and monitored for unintended harm. Keep language centered on policy in structure design: what gets processed first, who benefits, and what trade-off is accepted.

studentExercises.title: Stack vs Queue Demonstration
studentExercises.description: Use books and everyday examples to compare LIFO and FIFO processing behavior.
instructions: Build a physical stack and retrieve the bottom item.
State why last-added item is removed first in a stack.
Match undo history and browser back button to LIFO.
Compare with printer queue FIFO behavior.
Give one scenario where FIFO is fair but still unsafe.
successCriteria: Students correctly define LIFO and FIFO.
Students map each structure to an appropriate everyday use case.
Students identify at least one high-stakes FIFO limitation.

studentExercises.title: Priority Queue Basics
studentExercises.description: Translate triage scenario into severity-based ordering with explicit tie-breaking.
instructions: Order patients A to D by severity score.
Define tie-break rule when severity scores are equal.
Explain why Patient D can be first despite latest arrival.
Write one sentence on fairness trade-off for Patient A.
State what metadata your algorithm needs to maintain consistency.
successCriteria: Group outputs correct initial triage ordering.

studentExercises.title: Dynamic Reordering and Preemption
studentExercises.description: Handle new arrivals, score updates, and in-progress treatment constraints in a dynamic queue.
instructions: Insert Patient E with severity 7 into current queue.
Update Patient E to severity 10 and recompute order.
Decide whether algorithm preempts ongoing treatment of Patient B.
Specify what system-state information algorithm must read.
Document one risk introduced by your preemption policy.
successCriteria: Group handles dynamic insertion and score updates correctly.
Group articulates a clear preemption policy.
Group identifies required system state beyond queue contents.

studentExercises.title: Exit Reflection — Power and Responsibility
studentExercises.description: Write one sentence connecting priority queue power to accountability in weight-setting.
instructions: Complete: A priority queue is more powerful than a standard queue, but that power comes with the responsibility to _______.
Include who sets weights and how outcomes should be checked.
Share one response with class.
Revise for precision if sentence is vague.
successCriteria: Student links technical choice to accountability.
Student identifies at least one governance action.
Sentence reflects potential for unjust outcomes.

studentChallenges.title: Bias Without Explicit Variable
studentChallenges.description: Explore how triage outputs can become biased even when postcode is not an explicit model input.
instructions: Propose one indirect pathway by which postcode-correlated patterns could influence severity scores.
State one auditing method to detect this pattern.
Suggest one remediation path: fix, replace, or governance change.
Explain trade-offs of your chosen remediation.

studentChallenges.title: Redundancy Budget Decision
studentChallenges.description: Choose which priority-queue pipeline components deserve redundancy under limited resources.
instructions: Name two functions that should have immediate failover.
Name one function that can tolerate downtime.
Justify decisions using patient-risk impact.
Identify who in an organization should approve this trade-off.
successCriteria: Student prioritizes redundancy by consequence severity.
Student differentiates critical and non-critical components.

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these six words on the board — leave them visible all lesson.
Write or project the triage scenario on the board:
Patient A — arrived 9:00, severity 2, sprained ankle
Patient B — arrived 9:05, severity 9, chest pain
Patient C — arrived 9:10, severity 5, broken arm
Patient D — arrived 9:15, severity 10, cannot breathe
Have four books or notebooks stacked at the front for the stack demo.
Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
boardWords: Stack: a data structure where the last item added is the first one removed (LIFO)
Queue: a data structure where the first item added is the first one removed (FIFO)
LIFO: Last In, First Out — the stack processing order
FIFO: First In, First Out — the queue processing order
Priority Queue: a queue where items are processed by importance, not arrival order
Severity score: a number representing how urgent or critical a case is

lessonSlides.title: Annie Easley
lessonSlides.roleModel: Annie Easley (1933–2011) was a computer scientist at NASA for over 34 years. She started as a 'human computer' — manually computing math for researchers — and transitioned to programming when NASA adopted electronic computers. She developed code for energy-conversion systems and the Centaur rocket stage, which was used to launch communication satellites and interplanetary probes including the Cassini mission to Saturn. In her work, processing order was not a convenience — it was a safety requirement. The wrong sequence of computations in a rocket engine could mean the difference between a successful launch and a catastrophic failure. Annie also faced significant racial discrimination throughout her career — she was one of only four African American employees when she started at NASA's predecessor, NACA. She persevered, became a leading programmer, and mentored younger engineers. Her career demonstrates that the logic behind ordering and prioritisation is never just technical — it shapes real outcomes for real people.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Introduce Annie simply: 'Today's role model worked at NASA for 34 years. Her name is Annie Easley. She wrote code that helped launch rockets and satellites — including missions to Saturn. In her work, the order in which a computer processed tasks was not a small detail. Getting it wrong could destroy a rocket. Today, you are going to learn about the same thing — how systems decide what to process first, and why that matters.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Annie Easley's work on the Centaur rocket is still in use today — updated versions of the stage she programmed continue to launch missions. Her career also highlights that technical excellence does not protect against systemic discrimination. She had to fight for equal treatment while doing work that literally launched spacecraft. When students think about who sets the priorities in a system, Annie's story reminds them that the people behind the code matter as much as the code itself.

lessonSlides.title: What We're Doing Today
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Welcome the class and set the frame: 'Today we are going to learn how computers decide what to do first — and why that decision is more important than most people realise. You are going to learn three data structures: a stack, a queue, and a priority queue. Then you are going to use one of them to design an emergency triage system — where the order in which patients are treated can save or cost lives.'
whatToDo: Point to the vocabulary on the board: 'These six words are what we're working with today. By the end you will use all of them.'
Point to the triage scenario: 'Those four patients are waiting in an emergency room. You are going to decide the order they are treated in — and justify why.'
Let the weight of the scenario land. This is not abstract — students understand that hospitals make life-and-death ordering decisions.
This lesson is 30 minutes. Keep transitions sharp.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Ask the class: 'A self-driving car is approaching a junction. At the same moment, three things happen: a traffic light turns red, a pedestrian steps into the road, and the car behind brakes suddenly. The car's computer can only process one thing at a time. Which one does it handle first — and who decided that?'
whatToDo: Give students 15 seconds to think.
Take 2–3 quick answers.

Most will say the pedestrian — push: 'Why? Because a human life matters more than a traffic rule? That is a priority decision. Someone programmed that priority. Someone decided that a pedestrian outranks a red light.'
Say: 'Every computer makes ordering decisions — which task first, which email first, which patient first. Today we are going to learn the structures that make those decisions, and who controls them.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the class is quiet, try: 'Think about your phone. Right now it is doing dozens of things — checking for messages, updating the clock, listening for notifications. Which task gets priority? Who decided? You never set those rules — but someone did.'

lessonSlides.title: What Is a Stack?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'I am going to show you the first data structure. It is called a stack. Watch carefully.'
whatToDo: Pick up four books. Stack them on the table one by one, saying the title or colour of each.
Say: 'Now I need the first book I put down — the one at the bottom. Can I get it without moving the others?'
Demonstrate: you have to remove the top books one by one to reach the bottom.
Say: 'This is a stack. The last item you add is the first one you remove. We call this LIFO — Last In, First Out.'
Write STACK and LIFO on the board.
Ask: 'Where do you use a stack in everyday life?'
Guide to: the Undo button (Ctrl+Z) — the last action you did is the first one undone. And the browser Back button — the last page you visited is the first one you go back to.
Say: 'Stacks are useful when you need to reverse order or undo actions. But they have a problem — if something urgent is buried at the bottom, you cannot reach it quickly.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students look blank on real-world stacks, try: 'Think about a stack of plates in a cupboard. You always take the one on top. The one at the bottom — the one you put there first — might sit there for months. That is LIFO.'

lessonSlides.title: What Is a Queue?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now the second structure. This one you already know — you use it every day. It is called a queue.'
whatToDo: Say: 'Think about a queue at the supermarket. Who gets served first?'
Students: 'The person who arrived first.'
Say: 'Exactly. A queue processes items in the order they arrive. The first item in is the first one out. We call this FIFO — First In, First Out.'
Write QUEUE and FIFO on the board.
Give examples: 'A printer queue prints in the order you send documents. A message queue delivers messages in the order they arrive.'
Now the critical question: 'FIFO seems fair. Everyone waits their turn. But is it always safe?'

Pause. Then: 'Imagine you are in a hospital waiting room. You arrived at 9:00 with a sprained ankle. At 9:15, someone arrives who cannot breathe. Should they wait behind you because you arrived first?'
Students will say no.
Say: 'That is the problem with FIFO in high-stakes systems. Fairness of arrival order is not the same as fairness of urgency. We need something more sophisticated.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students say FIFO is always fair, push: 'Is it fair that a person with chest pain waits 45 minutes because 20 people with minor injuries arrived before them? FIFO treats everyone equally — but equal treatment is not always fair treatment.'

lessonSlides.title: What Is a Priority Queue?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'We need a structure that does not just process things in arrival order. We need one that processes by importance. That structure is called a priority queue.'
whatToDo: Write PRIORITY QUEUE on the board.
Say: 'In a priority queue, every item has a score — a number that says how urgent or important it is. The item with the highest score gets processed first, regardless of when it arrived.'
Point to the triage scenario on the board:
Patient A: severity 2 (sprained ankle) — arrived first at 9:00
Patient D: severity 10 (cannot breathe) — arrived last at 9:15
Say: 'In a FIFO queue, Patient A goes first. In a priority queue, Patient D goes first — because severity 10 beats severity 2, even though D arrived 15 minutes later.'
Ask: 'What happens when two patients have the same severity score?'

Guide: 'You need a tie-breaking rule. The most common one: if severity is equal, use arrival time — whoever arrived first goes first. But you could also use age, condition type, or any other rule. The tie-breaker is a policy choice.'
Ask: 'What happens if a new patient arrives with severity 10 while you are treating a severity-5 patient? Do you stop treating the 5 and switch to the 10?'

Say: 'That is called preemption — interrupting a current task for a higher-priority one. In hospitals, sometimes yes. In rocket systems — Annie Easley's world — absolutely yes. In a printer queue? Probably not. Whether to preempt is a design decision.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Emergency rooms worldwide use triage systems based on priority queues. The Manchester Triage System assigns every patient a colour-coded urgency level. Red (immediate) always goes before yellow (urgent), which goes before green (standard). Arrival time only matters within the same colour. The weights and categories are set by medical consensus — but they are still human decisions encoded into an algorithm.
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Annie Easley's rocket processing code treated execution order as a safety requirement, not a preference. A priority queue is not just a data structure — it is a statement about what matters most, encoded as logic.

lessonSlides.title: Stack vs Queue Demonstration
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Let's make sure you understand both structures. Stack: LIFO. Queue: FIFO. Give me one real-world example of each — and one case where FIFO fairness becomes dangerous.'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 2.
Use the physical book stack to anchor LIFO before moving to abstract examples.
Push students to find the dangerous FIFO case — that transition motivates Phase 3.

lessonSlides.title: Triage Challenge — Initial Ordering
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now it is your turn. In groups of three, look at the four patients on the board. Using severity scores, put them in the correct treatment order. You have 3 minutes.'
whatToDo: Form groups of three.
Groups should produce the order: D (10), B (9), C (5), A (2).
After 2 minutes, check answers. Most groups will get this right.
Push the fairness question: 'Patient A arrived at 9:00. Patient D arrived at 9:15. Patient A has been waiting the longest — but they are treated last. Is that fair?'

Guide: 'It depends on what you mean by fair. Fair-by-arrival says A goes first. Fair-by-urgency says D goes first. They cannot both be satisfied. A priority queue makes a choice — and that choice has consequences for Patient A.'
Say: 'Patient A has a sprained ankle and waits the longest. That is the cost of a priority queue. Someone always waits longer than they would under FIFO. The question is: is that cost justified?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups disagree about fairness, that is good. Ask: 'Who thinks Patient A is being treated unfairly? Who thinks the system is correct? Both positions are valid — that is why this is a design question, not a math question.'

lessonSlides.title: Priority Queue Basics
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Order patients A to D by severity. Define your tie-break rule. Explain why Patient D goes first despite arriving last.'
rules: Order by severity score — highest first.
Define a tie-break rule for equal severity scores.
Justify why arrival order is overridden.
Write one sentence on the fairness cost for Patient A.
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 4 Round 1.
See the Phase 4 Round 1 slide for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Dynamic Update — Patient E
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'New situation. A new patient arrives. And then things change.'
whatToDo: Write on the board: Patient E — arrived 9:20, severity 7, deep cut with heavy bleeding.
Say: 'Insert Patient E into your queue. Where does she go?'
Give groups 1 minute. E should go after B (9) and before C (5): order is D (10), B (9), E (7), C (5), A (2).
Now the twist: 'Patient E's bleeding gets worse. The doctor re-assesses her severity from 7 to 10. What happens to your queue?'
Give groups 2 minutes.
There are now two severity-10 patients: D and E. Groups need a tie-break. If tie-break is arrival time, D stays first (arrived 9:15 vs E at 9:20).
Now the hard question: 'Patient B is currently being treated — severity 9. Patient E is now severity 10. Do you stop treating B and switch to E?'

Say: 'That is called preemption. In a hospital, stopping treatment mid-procedure can be dangerous. But ignoring a severity-10 patient can also be fatal. There is no clean answer — only a policy that you design and defend.'
Walk around. Push groups to write a clear preemption rule: under what conditions is preemption allowed?
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups avoid the preemption question, force it: 'Patient B is having a blood test taken. Patient E cannot breathe. Do you stop the blood test? What if Patient B is mid-surgery? Does the answer change? Where is the line?'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Easley's code handled dynamic reordering in real time — new information changed the execution priority mid-run. Your preemption logic is that same principle: the queue is never static.

lessonSlides.title: Dynamic Reordering and Preemption
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Insert Patient E. Handle the severity update. Decide your preemption policy. Write it down clearly — a vague rule is a dangerous rule.'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 4 Round 2.
See the Phase 4 Round 2 slide for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Tie-Break Stress Test
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final round. Six new patients arrive at exactly the same time — 9:25. All of them are severity 8. Your algorithm must decide who goes first. How?'
whatToDo: Give groups 2 minutes to define a tie-breaking rule.
Push: 'Arrival time is the same for all six. You cannot use it. What else?'
Possible tie-breakers: age (youngest first?), condition type (breathing over bleeding?), random assignment, alphabetical (arbitrary but deterministic).

Ask: 'Every tie-break rule is a value judgment. If you choose youngest-first, you are saying children's lives are worth more. If you choose random, you are saying fairness means equal chance. None of these are neutral.'
Close: 'An algorithm without a defined tie-break is an algorithm with a bug. Unspecified behavior means inconsistent outcomes — and in a hospital, that is dangerous.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups pick 'random', ask: 'Is random fair? What if one of the six patients is a 90-year-old and another is a 5-year-old? Should their chances really be equal? Or should age matter? That is not a technical question — it is a moral one.'

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than two rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: 'Where is preemption clearly the right decision — and where is it dangerous?' Follow-up: 'A 999 call interrupts a non-emergency police task. That is preemption. But what if a social media platform preempts your feed to show you an ad? Same mechanism — different ethics.'
'Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube all use priority queues to decide what you see next. Who sets the weights? Is engagement a good priority score — or does it prioritise the wrong things?'
'What is the difference between a hospital triage queue and a social media feed? Both use priority scoring. Both affect people's lives. Are they ethically the same?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If discussion is slow, try: 'Your social media feed is a priority queue. The algorithm decides: this post goes first, this one goes last. The weights are set by the company. You never agreed to those weights. Is that acceptable?'

lessonSlides.title: Exit Reflection — Power and Responsibility
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final task. On your own, complete this sentence: A priority queue is more powerful than a standard queue, but that power comes with the responsibility to _______.'

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask every student to write and complete the sentence on paper or sticky note.
They must include who sets the weights and how outcomes should be checked.
Collect papers or stick them on the board.
Read 2–3 strong responses aloud.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: A priority queue is more powerful than a standard queue, but that power comes with the responsibility to _______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Close with: 'Annie Easley spent 34 years making sure computers processed the right thing at the right time. You just designed a system that does the same. Next lesson, you will learn what happens when computers learn patterns from data — and what goes wrong when the data itself has problems.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If reflections are shallow, push: 'Do not just say be fair. Be specific. Who sets the weights? How do you know the weights are correct? What happens when they are wrong? Who is responsible for checking?'
